---
title: "A Course in Random Matrix Theory"
subtitle: "Eigenvalue repulsion, the semicircle law, and high-dimensional spectral statistics"
author: "Charles Renshaw-Whitman"
date: today
bibliography: references.bib
execute:
  cache: true
  freeze: auto
---

::: {.callout-note}
## What you will get from this tutorial

A working understanding of why classical probability fails when many degrees of freedom are coupled in a generic way, and what random matrix theory predicts in its place.  We derive the joint eigenvalue density from a Jacobian, reframe it as a log-gas, solve for the semicircle two different ways (variational and resolvent), do the same for Marchenko-Pastur, and end with universality, the Tracy-Widom edge, and what all of this is good for.

This is meant to be used as a course.  Read the derivations.  Do the exercises.  Slide every slider.
:::

## 1.  The high-dimensional surprise

You measure $p$ features on $n$ samples, each row drawn iid from a multivariate Gaussian with **true covariance** $\Sigma = I$.  You compute the **sample covariance** $\hat{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n} X^\top X$ where $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$.  Classical statistics says $\hat{\Sigma} \to I$ as $n \to \infty$, so the eigenvalues of $\hat{\Sigma}$ should pile up at $1$.

Watch what happens when $p$ scales with $n$.  Let $\gamma = p / n$ be the **concentration ratio** and slide it.

```{ojs}
//| label: fig-cov-spectra
//| fig-cap: "Histogram of sample-covariance eigenvalues for iid Gaussian features with true covariance $\\Sigma = I$.  Blue: empirical histogram across 20 realizations.  Orange: the analytic Marchenko-Pastur density.  Density is normalized so $\\int \\rho(\\lambda) d\\lambda = 1$ over the bulk; when $\\gamma > 1$ there is additional point mass $1 - 1/\\gamma$ at $\\lambda = 0$ that we do not draw."

cov = (await fetch("data/section1_cov.json")).json()

viewof gammaIdx = Inputs.range([0, cov.gammas.length - 1], {
  step: 1,
  value: 9,
  label: "γ = p / n (concentration ratio)",
  format: i => cov.gammas[i].toFixed(2)
})

panel = cov.panels[cov.gammas[gammaIdx].toFixed(2)]

Plot.plot({
  title: `Sample-covariance spectrum at γ = ${panel.gamma}  (p = ${panel.p}, n = ${panel.n})`,
  width: 720,
  height: 340,
  marginLeft: 60,
  marginBottom: 50,
  x: {label: "eigenvalue λ", domain: [0, Math.max(...panel.mp_x)]},
  y: {label: "density ρ(λ)", grid: true},
  style: {background: "transparent", color: "#d8d6cf", fontSize: "13px"},
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(
      panel.hist_x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: panel.hist_y[i]})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "#6cb0ff", fillOpacity: 0.55, interval: panel.hist_x[1] - panel.hist_x[0]}
    ),
    Plot.line(
      panel.mp_x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: panel.mp_y[i]})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#f0c040", strokeWidth: 2.4}
    ),
    Plot.ruleX([panel.lam_minus, panel.lam_plus], {stroke: "#ff6c8e", strokeDasharray: "3,3"}),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Try this
Slide $\gamma$ from $0.05$ up to $1$.  Watch the support of the eigenvalue density widen from a tight cluster around $1$ to the interval $[0, 4]$.  At $\gamma = 1$ the lower edge $\lambda_- = (1 - \sqrt{\gamma})^2$ touches zero — the sample covariance is barely invertible.  Push past $\gamma = 1$: the sample covariance is now singular ($n - p$ exact zeros), and the bulk slides to the right.
:::

The classical story (eigenvalues pile up at $1$) is *only* correct in the limit $p$ fixed, $n \to \infty$.  In modern problems where $p$ and $n$ both grow, the limit on the screen is the right description.  The orange curve is the **Marchenko-Pastur density**

$$
\rho_{\text{MP}}(\lambda) \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{(\lambda_+ - \lambda)(\lambda - \lambda_-)}}{2\pi\, \gamma\, \lambda},
\qquad \lambda \in [\lambda_-, \lambda_+], \quad \lambda_\pm = (1 \pm \sqrt{\gamma})^2.
$$

We will derive this from scratch in §6.  For now, take it as evidence: high-dimensional intuition is not a small correction to the classical story.  It is a different theory.

::: {.callout-important}
## Exercise 1.1
At $\gamma = 1$, the smallest eigenvalue of the sample covariance is approximately zero.  This is the source of the standard advice "you need $n \gg p$ for stable covariance estimation."  Use the formula above to determine: at $\gamma = 0.5$, what is the ratio $\lambda_+ / \lambda_-$?  This is roughly the **condition number** of $\hat{\Sigma}$ -- a measure of how badly inverting it amplifies noise.

<details><summary>Solution</summary>

$\lambda_+ = (1 + \sqrt{0.5})^2 \approx 2.914$, $\lambda_- = (1 - \sqrt{0.5})^2 \approx 0.086$.  Ratio $\approx 34$.  So even at $\gamma = 1/2$ the sample covariance is over an order of magnitude more ill-conditioned than the true $\Sigma = I$.  Inverting $\hat{\Sigma}$ to estimate, say, a precision matrix or a regression coefficient amplifies noise by factor 34.

</details>
:::

The rest of the tutorial explains why this happens, and what the right replacement for classical eigenvalue intuition is.

## 2.  Eigenvalue repulsion is the key

The Marchenko-Pastur density is one instance of a far broader phenomenon.  The deepest fact about eigenvalues of large random matrices is that **they repel each other**.  Two eigenvalues do not like to sit close together; the probability that they coincide goes to zero as a power of the gap.  This single feature is what distinguishes RMT from classical probability, and everything in §3-§7 follows from it.

To make this precise we need the **joint eigenvalue density**.  Start with the simplest interesting ensemble:

::: {.callout-note}
## Definition: Gaussian Unitary Ensemble (GUE)
$H$ is a complex $N \times N$ Hermitian matrix.  Its diagonal entries are real Gaussians; its off-diagonal entries are complex Gaussians.  Variances are tuned so that

$$
P(H) \;\propto\; \exp\!\left(-\tfrac{N}{2}\,\mathrm{Tr}\,H^2\right).
$$

This is the unique probability measure on Hermitian matrices that is (a) invariant under unitary conjugation $H \mapsto U H U^\dagger$ and (b) has independent entries.
:::

The "U" in GUE refers to *unitary* invariance.  The two real-symmetric and quaternion-self-dual analogs (GOE and GSE) replace unitary with orthogonal and symplectic invariance respectively; they show up in §7.

### From entries to eigenvalues: the Vandermonde

The matrix $H$ has $N^2$ real degrees of freedom.  We are not interested in all of them, only the $N$ eigenvalues.  The unitary frame holding those eigenvalues in a particular basis ($N^2 - N$ degrees of freedom) is irrelevant.  So we change variables.

Diagonalize: $H = U \Lambda U^\dagger$ with $\Lambda = \mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_N)$.  We want the joint density of $(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_N)$, after integrating out $U$.  By unitary invariance, the marginal over $U$ is the Haar measure, and integrating it out just contributes a constant.  The non-trivial piece is the **Jacobian** of the change of variables from $H$ to $(U, \Lambda)$.

Working it out (see Mehta @mehta2004, ch. 3) gives the celebrated result:

$$
dH \;=\; |\Delta(\boldsymbol{\lambda})|^2 \, d\boldsymbol{\lambda} \, d\mu_{\text{Haar}}(U), \qquad \Delta(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) \;\equiv\; \prod_{i < j}(\lambda_i - \lambda_j).
$$

Here $\Delta$ is the **Vandermonde determinant**.  The square shows up because GUE is a *complex* matrix model; for real symmetric (GOE) the power is $1$, and for quaternion (GSE) the power is $4$.  Combining with the Boltzmann weight gives the joint density

$$
\boxed{
P(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_N) \;\propto\; \prod_{i < j} |\lambda_i - \lambda_j|^2 \,\cdot\, \prod_{i} e^{-\frac{N}{2}\lambda_i^2}.
}
$$

Stare at this for a moment.  The Vandermonde-squared factor vanishes whenever any two eigenvalues coincide, *quadratically*.  Two eigenvalues sitting at distance $s$ from each other contribute a factor $s^2$ to the joint density.  Coincidences are not just unlikely, they are smoothly forbidden.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Where does the $\Delta^2$ come from, intuitively?
The pair $(U, \Lambda)$ does not parametrize the matrix $H$ uniquely: any permutation of $\{\lambda_i\}$ together with the matching column reordering of $U$ gives the same $H$.  Worse, when two eigenvalues coincide there is a continuous $U(2)$ rotation in the corresponding two-dimensional eigenspace that leaves $H$ unchanged.  The Jacobian must vanish at coincident eigenvalues to cancel this redundancy in the parametrization.  The vanishing rate is $|\lambda_i - \lambda_j|^{\beta}$ with $\beta \in \{1, 2, 4\}$ tracking the dimension of the relevant rotation group ($O(2)$, $U(2)$, $Sp(2)$).
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Exercise 2.1
The density above is invariant under reordering of the $\lambda_i$.  So strictly speaking it is the density of an *unordered* set.  Ordered eigenvalues $\lambda_1 < \lambda_2 < \cdots$ get an extra factor of $N!$ from the Jacobian.  Verify this and compute the partition function $Z$ — the normalization constant — for $N = 2$ in the GUE case.

<details><summary>Solution</summary>

For ordered eigenvalues there is one ordering out of $N!$, so the ordered density is $N!$ times the unordered.  For $N=2$, GUE:

$$
Z = \int_{\mathbb{R}^2} (\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)^2 \, e^{-(\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2)} \, d\lambda_1 \, d\lambda_2.
$$

Switch to centre and gap variables $u = (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)/\sqrt{2}$, $v = (\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)/\sqrt{2}$.  The Jacobian is 1 and the integrand factors:

$$
Z = \int e^{-u^2} du \cdot \int (\sqrt{2} v)^2 e^{-v^2} dv = \sqrt{\pi} \cdot 2 \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} = \pi.
$$

</details>
:::

### Test: spacings vanish at zero

The cleanest experimental test of repulsion: pick the gaps $s_i = \lambda_{i+1} - \lambda_i$ between consecutive sorted eigenvalues, normalize by their mean, and histogram.  For iid samples from any continuous distribution, this gives the **Poisson** spacing distribution $p(s) = e^{-s}$ — a flat, *non-zero* value at $s = 0$.  Two samples can sit on top of each other.  For GUE eigenvalues, the histogram should vanish at $s = 0$.

```{ojs}
//| label: fig-spacings
//| fig-cap: "Mean-normalized spacing histograms.  Left: $N = 20{,}000$ iid uniform samples; the density at small $s$ is positive (Poisson, $p(s) = e^{-s}$).  Right: spacings between consecutive eigenvalues from the central half of $200$ realizations of GUE matrices of size $N = 300$; the density vanishes quadratically at $s = 0$ (Wigner surmise, $p(s) = (32/\\pi^2)\\, s^2 e^{-4 s^2/\\pi}$).  Both panels are normalized so $\\int p(s) ds = 1$ and $\\langle s \\rangle = 1$."

spacings = (await fetch("data/section2_spacings.json")).json()

spacings_combined = [
  ...spacings.poisson.x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: spacings.poisson.y[i], kind: "iid samples (Poisson)", series: "hist"})),
  ...spacings.gue.x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: spacings.gue.y[i], kind: "GUE eigenvalues (Wigner surmise)", series: "hist"})),
  ...spacings.exp_x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: spacings.exp_y[i], kind: "iid samples (Poisson)", series: "line"})),
  ...spacings.wigner_x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: spacings.wigner_y[i], kind: "GUE eigenvalues (Wigner surmise)", series: "line"}))
]

Plot.plot({
  title: "Spacings: classical (Poisson) vs RMT (Wigner)",
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 60,
  marginBottom: 50,
  x: {label: "spacing s / ⟨s⟩", domain: [0, 4]},
  y: {label: "density p(s)", grid: true, domain: [0, 1.1]},
  fx: {label: null},
  style: {background: "transparent", color: "#d8d6cf", fontSize: "13px"},
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(
      spacings_combined.filter(d => d.series === "hist"),
      {x: "x", y: "y", fx: "kind", fill: "#6cb0ff", fillOpacity: 0.55,
       interval: spacings.poisson.x[1] - spacings.poisson.x[0]}
    ),
    Plot.line(
      spacings_combined.filter(d => d.series === "line"),
      {x: "x", y: "y", fx: "kind", stroke: "#f0c040", strokeWidth: 2.4}
    ),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```

The orange curves are the analytic limits.  The fact that the GUE histogram drops as $s^2$ near zero is repulsion in pure form: the $\Delta^2$ factor in the joint density forces the gap distribution to vanish quadratically.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Try this
Look at the GUE panel near $s = 0$ and squint.  The histogram is unmistakably parabolic, not flat.  This single empirical signature is enough to detect that an eigenvalue spectrum is not iid samples.  In fact, a famous application: the spacings between zeros of the Riemann zeta function on the critical line follow the GUE Wigner surmise, leading Hugh Montgomery and Freeman Dyson to conjecture in 1973 a deep connection between number theory and random matrix theory.
:::

::: {.column-margin}
The exact distribution of spacings in the limit $N \to \infty$ is *not* the Wigner surmise — it involves a Painlevé V function.  But the surmise (computed for $N = 2$) is so close to the truth that nobody outside specialist circles bothers with the exact answer.  See Mehta @mehta2004 ch. 6.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Key idea
The Vandermonde-squared factor $\prod_{i<j}|\lambda_i - \lambda_j|^2$ in the joint density is the single feature that distinguishes RMT eigenvalues from classical iid samples.  Everything that follows -- the semicircle, Marchenko-Pastur, Tracy-Widom -- is downstream of this one fact.
:::

## 3.  The eigenvalues form a log-gas

The joint density is a Boltzmann distribution.  Reading it that way unlocks the entire stat-mech toolkit: free-energy variational principles, saddle-point approximations, mean-field equations, fluctuation theory.  All we have to do is take the log and read off the Hamiltonian.

$$
-\log P(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) \;=\; \frac{N}{2}\sum_i \lambda_i^2 \;-\; 2 \sum_{i<j} \log|\lambda_i - \lambda_j| \;+\; \text{const}.
$$

This is the energy of $N$ classical particles on the real line, with two contributions:

- **A confining potential** $V(\lambda) = \frac{1}{2}\lambda^2$ that pulls each particle toward the origin.
- **A pairwise logarithmic repulsion** $-\log|\lambda_i - \lambda_j|$ between every pair of particles.

This is the **log-gas** (or **Coulomb gas in two dimensions confined to a line**).  Logarithmic repulsion is the 2D Coulomb potential, and the eigenvalues sit on the line as if they were point charges trapped on a 1D wire embedded in a 2D world.

### N as inverse temperature

The Boltzmann form is $P \propto e^{-\beta H}$ for some inverse temperature $\beta$.  Where is $\beta$ here?  Look at the scaling.  The first sum is $O(N)$; the second is $O(N^2)$; the typical eigenvalue is $O(1)$ in the Wigner normalization.  Pull $N$ out front:

$$
-\log P(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) \;=\; N^2 \left[\frac{1}{2N}\sum_i \frac{\lambda_i^2}{2}\cdot 2 \;-\; \frac{1}{N^2}\sum_{i<j} \log|\lambda_i - \lambda_j| \right] + \text{const}.
$$

Both bracketed pieces are $O(1)$ functionals of the empirical measure $\mu_N = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \delta_{\lambda_i}$.  The prefactor $N^2$ is the inverse temperature.  $N \to \infty$ is the *cold* limit: fluctuations away from the minimum-energy configuration cost $\exp(-N^2 \cdot \text{stuff})$, which is exponentially small.

This is the central physical insight of RMT.  Large random matrices are *not* random in their bulk behavior.  They are deterministic, with the determinism coming from a free-energy minimization principle.  The randomness is confined to small fluctuations near the minimum.

::: {.callout-important}
## Exercise 3.1
For two GUE eigenvalues ($N=2$), compute the most probable separation analytically.  This is the saddle point of the energy with respect to $|\lambda_1 - \lambda_2|$ at fixed center of mass.

<details><summary>Solution</summary>

With $u = (\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)/2$ fixed, write $v = \lambda_1 - \lambda_2$.  Then $\lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 = 2u^2 + v^2/2$.  The energy as a function of $v$ alone is

$$
E(v) = \frac{N}{2}\cdot \frac{v^2}{2} - 2 \log|v| + \text{const}.
$$

For $N = 2$: $\frac{dE}{dv} = v - 2/v = 0 \implies v^2 = 2 \implies |v| = \sqrt{2}$.  So two GUE eigenvalues most probably sit a distance $\sqrt{2}$ apart in the standard normalization.  This is exactly what you would compute from the joint density $P(v) \propto v^2 e^{-v^2/2}$ for the gap.

</details>
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Key idea
$N$ plays the role of inverse temperature in the log-gas.  As $N \to \infty$ the eigenvalue distribution freezes into the unique configuration that minimizes the free energy.
:::

## 4.  The semicircle from a saddle point

In the cold limit the integral over eigenvalue configurations is dominated by the single density $\rho$ that minimizes the free-energy functional.  Replace the empirical measure $\frac{1}{N}\sum_i \delta_{\lambda_i}$ by a smooth density $\rho(\lambda)$.  The energy per eigenvalue becomes

$$
F[\rho] \;=\; \int V(\lambda)\, \rho(\lambda)\, d\lambda \;-\; \iint \rho(\lambda)\, \rho(\mu)\, \log|\lambda - \mu|\, d\lambda\, d\mu,
$$

with $V(\lambda) = \frac{1}{2}\lambda^2$ for GUE.  We want the $\rho$ that minimizes $F$ subject to $\int \rho = 1$.

### The saddle-point equation

Introduce a Lagrange multiplier $\mu_*$ for the normalization and differentiate $F[\rho] - \mu_* \int \rho$ with respect to $\rho(\lambda)$ pointwise.  The derivative of $\int V \rho$ is just $V(\lambda)$.  The derivative of the double integral, using symmetry, is $-2 \int \rho(\mu) \log|\lambda - \mu| d\mu$.  Setting the derivative to zero:

$$
V(\lambda) \;-\; 2 \int \rho(\mu) \log|\lambda - \mu|\, d\mu \;=\; \mu_*, \qquad \lambda \in \mathrm{supp}(\rho).
$$

Differentiate once more in $\lambda$ to kill the constant.  $V'(\lambda) = \lambda$, and the derivative of $\log|\lambda - \mu|$ is $1/(\lambda - \mu)$ (in the principal-value sense, since the integrand has a singularity at $\mu = \lambda$):

$$
\boxed{
\mathrm{P.V.}\!\int \frac{\rho(\mu)}{\lambda - \mu}\, d\mu \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{2}, \qquad \lambda \in \mathrm{supp}(\rho).
}
$$

The left side is the **Hilbert transform** of $\rho$.  The equation says: at every $\lambda$ in the support of the density, the Hilbert transform must equal $\lambda/2$.  This is a singular integral equation; we need to invert it.

### Tricomi inversion

Assume $\rho$ is supported on a single interval $[-a, a]$ symmetric about zero (justified by the symmetry of $V$).  The **Tricomi inversion formula** for the finite-Hilbert-transform on $[-a, a]$ gives

$$
\rho(\lambda) \;=\; \frac{1}{\pi^2 \sqrt{a^2 - \lambda^2}} \left( C \;-\; \mathrm{P.V.}\!\int_{-a}^{a} \frac{\sqrt{a^2 - \mu^2}}{\lambda - \mu} \cdot \frac{\mu}{2}\, d\mu \right),
$$

where $C$ is fixed by normalization.  The integral evaluates to $-\frac{\pi}{2}(\lambda^2 - a^2/2)$.  Substituting and demanding $\int \rho = 1$ pins $a = 2$ and $C = 0$, leaving

$$
\boxed{
\rho_{\text{sc}}(\lambda) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{4 - \lambda^2}, \qquad \lambda \in [-2, 2].
}
$$

This is the **Wigner semicircle**.  The eigenvalues of a large GUE matrix do not pile up at the bottom of the quadratic confining potential (which would be a Gaussian, by analogy with a single Brownian particle).  They spread out into a *semicircular* density, because pairwise repulsion pushes them apart.

::: {.column-margin}
For other confining potentials $V(\lambda)$, the same Tricomi machinery produces other densities.  $V \propto \lambda^4$ gives a "double-well" eigenvalue support.  $V \propto |\lambda|$ gives an arcsine-shaped density.  These show up in lattice gauge theory and 2D quantum gravity.  Mehta @mehta2004 ch. 4 has the calculations.
:::

### Watch the convergence

We just derived the limit.  How fast do real GUE matrices get there?

```{ojs}
//| label: fig-semicircle
//| fig-cap: "Eigenvalue histograms of GUE matrices of size $N$, with the analytic semicircle $\\rho(\\lambda) = \\frac{1}{2\\pi}\\sqrt{4 - \\lambda^2}$ overlaid in orange.  Density is normalized so $\\int \\rho = 1$.  Histograms aggregate enough realizations to keep the visual smooth (the count is shown above the plot)."

semi = (await fetch("data/section4_semicircle.json")).json()

viewof N_choice = Inputs.range([0, semi.Ns.length - 1], {
  step: 1,
  value: 3,
  label: "N (matrix size)",
  format: i => semi.Ns[i]
})

semiPanel = semi.panels[String(semi.Ns[N_choice])]

Plot.plot({
  title: `GUE spectrum at N = ${semi.Ns[N_choice]} (averaged over ${semiPanel.n_real} realizations)`,
  width: 720,
  height: 340,
  marginLeft: 60,
  marginBottom: 50,
  x: {label: "eigenvalue λ", domain: [-2.6, 2.6]},
  y: {label: "density ρ(λ)", grid: true, domain: [0, 0.40]},
  style: {background: "transparent", color: "#d8d6cf", fontSize: "13px"},
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(
      semiPanel.x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: semiPanel.y[i]})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "#6cb0ff", fillOpacity: 0.55, interval: semiPanel.x[1] - semiPanel.x[0]}
    ),
    Plot.line(
      semi.semicircle_x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: semi.semicircle_y[i]})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#f0c040", strokeWidth: 2.4}
    ),
    Plot.ruleX([-2, 2], {stroke: "#ff6c8e", strokeDasharray: "3,3"}),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Try this
At $N = 10$ the histogram is wild noise around the orange curve.  By $N = 100$ it tracks the curve closely; by $N = 1000$ residuals are visible only at the edges (where finite-$N$ corrections live, in the form of edge oscillations on scale $N^{-2/3}$ -- see §8).  The *bulk* convergence is fast.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Exercise 4.1
Verify by direct computation that the Wigner semicircle $\rho_{\text{sc}}(\lambda) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{4 - \lambda^2}$ on $[-2, 2]$ is normalized to $1$ and has variance $\langle \lambda^2 \rangle = 1$.

<details><summary>Solution</summary>

Substitute $\lambda = 2\sin\theta$, $d\lambda = 2\cos\theta\, d\theta$, $\sqrt{4 - \lambda^2} = 2\cos\theta$.  Then

$$
\int_{-2}^{2} \frac{\sqrt{4 - \lambda^2}}{2\pi}\, d\lambda \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi/2}^{\pi/2} 4\cos^2\theta\, d\theta \;=\; \frac{4}{2\pi}\cdot \frac{\pi}{2} = 1.
$$

For the variance, $\langle \lambda^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-2}^{2}\lambda^2 \sqrt{4-\lambda^2}\, d\lambda$.  Same substitution: $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int 4\sin^2\theta \cdot 4\cos^2\theta\, d\theta = \frac{16}{2\pi}\cdot \frac{\pi}{8} = 1$.  So the typical eigenvalue is order one.  This justifies our normalization $H_{ij} \sim 1/\sqrt{N}$: it is the unique scaling at which eigenvalues are $O(1)$.

</details>
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Key idea
The Wigner semicircle is the unique density on the line that minimizes a 2D-Coulomb-plus-quadratic energy under unit normalization.  No randomness left -- it is a *deterministic* fact about confined log-gases.
:::

## 5.  The Stieltjes transform: a cleaner machine

Tricomi inversion works but it is an awkward calculation, and it does not generalize cleanly to two-cut supports or asymmetric potentials.  There is a cleaner tool: the **Stieltjes transform** (also called the resolvent, or Cauchy transform) of the eigenvalue density.

::: {.callout-note}
## Definition
For a probability density $\rho$ on $\mathbb{R}$, the Stieltjes transform is the function

$$
G(z) \;\equiv\; \int \frac{\rho(\lambda)}{z - \lambda}\, d\lambda, \qquad z \in \mathbb{C} \setminus \mathrm{supp}(\rho).
$$
:::

$G(z)$ is analytic off the real axis, decays as $1/z$ at infinity, and -- critically -- contains *all* the information about $\rho$.  Recover $\rho$ via the **Sokhotski-Plemelj inversion formula**:

$$
\rho(\lambda) \;=\; -\frac{1}{\pi}\, \mathrm{Im}\, G(\lambda + i 0^+).
$$

Approach the real axis from above; the imaginary part of $G$ is, up to a sign and a factor of $\pi$, the density.  This converts the problem of finding $\rho$ into the problem of finding $G$, which is often much easier because $G$ obeys algebraic equations.

### Watch the cut emerge

Below: $G(\lambda + i \varepsilon)$ for the Wigner semicircle, plotted as $-\mathrm{Im}\,G/\pi$.  As $\varepsilon \to 0$ the imaginary part collapses onto the semicircle support and traces out the density itself.  The "cut" of $G$ -- the discontinuity across $\mathrm{supp}(\rho)$ -- *is* the density.

```{ojs}
//| label: fig-stieltjes
//| fig-cap: "Imaginary part of the Stieltjes transform of the Wigner semicircle, $-\\mathrm{Im}\\,G(\\lambda + i\\varepsilon)/\\pi$, as $\\varepsilon$ shrinks toward zero.  The orange curve is the analytic semicircle density.  The blue curve, an analytic function on the upper half plane, smoothly limits to the (discontinuous-derivative) density on the support and to zero off it."

st = (await fetch("data/section_stieltjes.json")).json()

viewof epsIdx = Inputs.range([0, st.epsilons.length - 1], {
  step: 1,
  value: 2,
  label: "ε (imaginary offset of z = λ + iε)",
  format: i => st.epsilons[i].toFixed(4)
})

stPanel = st.panels[st.epsilons[epsIdx].toFixed(4)]

Plot.plot({
  title: `−Im G(λ + iε) / π   at ε = ${st.epsilons[epsIdx].toFixed(4)}`,
  width: 720,
  height: 340,
  marginLeft: 60,
  marginBottom: 50,
  x: {label: "λ", domain: [-3, 3]},
  y: {label: "−Im G / π", grid: true, domain: [-0.05, 0.40]},
  style: {background: "transparent", color: "#d8d6cf", fontSize: "13px"},
  marks: [
    Plot.line(
      stPanel.lam.map((x, i) => ({x, y: stPanel.neg_im_G_over_pi[i]})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#6cb0ff", strokeWidth: 2.4}
    ),
    Plot.line(
      st.semicircle_x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: st.semicircle_y[i]})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#f0c040", strokeWidth: 1.6, strokeDasharray: "5,3"}
    ),
    Plot.ruleX([-2, 2], {stroke: "#ff6c8e", strokeDasharray: "3,3"}),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Try this
Slide $\varepsilon$ from $0.5$ down to $0.001$.  At large $\varepsilon$ the blue curve is a smoothed-out broad bump.  As $\varepsilon$ shrinks, the bump tightens onto the semicircle support and its height grows to match the density exactly.  This is Sokhotski-Plemelj in action.
:::

### Re-deriving the semicircle, this time the easy way

For GUE there is a classical *self-consistent* equation that $G$ obeys.  Consider the resolvent of the matrix itself, $\hat{G}(z) = \frac{1}{N}\mathrm{Tr}\,(z I - H)^{-1}$.  This is the same as $\sum_i \frac{1}{N(z - \lambda_i)}$, so $\mathbb{E}\hat{G}(z) \to G(z)$ as $N \to \infty$.

A standard derivation (block matrix decomposition + Schur complement; see Tao's notes on free probability for a clean presentation, or any RMT textbook) shows that for GUE the Stieltjes transform satisfies

$$
G(z) \;=\; \frac{1}{z - G(z)},
\qquad \text{equivalently,} \qquad G^2 - z G + 1 = 0.
$$

This is the **self-consistent equation** for the GUE resolvent.  It comes from the loop "knock out one row+column, the remaining $(N-1) \times (N-1)$ matrix has the same self-averaging $G$, plus a correction that cancels in the limit."  Solve the quadratic:

$$
G(z) \;=\; \frac{z - \sqrt{z^2 - 4}}{2}.
$$

(Branch chosen so $G(z) \sim 1/z$ at infinity.)  Apply Sokhotski-Plemelj.  For real $\lambda \in (-2, 2)$, $z^2 - 4$ is negative, so $\sqrt{z^2 - 4 + i 0^+} = i\sqrt{4 - \lambda^2}$, and

$$
\rho(\lambda) \;=\; -\frac{1}{\pi}\mathrm{Im}\,G(\lambda + i 0^+) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{4 - \lambda^2}.
$$

Same semicircle, this time in three lines instead of three pages.  The Stieltjes-transform machine is the workhorse of modern RMT.

::: {.callout-important}
## Exercise 5.1
Outside the support, $\rho = 0$, so $G(\lambda)$ is purely real for $|\lambda| > 2$.  Use the explicit formula to verify this and compute $G(3)$ numerically.

<details><summary>Solution</summary>

For $\lambda > 2$, $\lambda^2 - 4 > 0$, so $\sqrt{\lambda^2 - 4}$ is real and $G$ is real.  At $\lambda = 3$: $G(3) = (3 - \sqrt{5})/2 \approx 0.382$.

</details>
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Key idea
The Stieltjes transform converts the integral equation for $\rho$ into an algebraic equation for $G$.  This is what makes RMT computations tractable.  Every result we did via Tricomi can be redone in two or three lines via Stieltjes.
:::

## 6.  Marchenko-Pastur

Now we can derive the orange curve from §1.  The setup: $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ with iid entries of mean zero and unit variance, $\hat{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n} X^\top X$, and $\gamma = p / n$.  We want the limiting eigenvalue density of $\hat{\Sigma}$ as $p, n \to \infty$ at fixed $\gamma$.

### Stieltjes derivation

Let $G(z)$ be the Stieltjes transform of the limiting eigenvalue density of $\hat{\Sigma}$.  An analogous block-matrix argument (see Bai-Silverstein for the canonical treatment, or any modern free-probability reference) produces the **Marchenko-Pastur self-consistent equation**:

$$
G(z) \;=\; \frac{1}{z - 1 + \gamma - \gamma z G(z)}.
$$

Cross-multiply:

$$
\gamma z G^2 \;-\; (z - 1 + \gamma)\, G \;+\; 1 \;=\; 0.
$$

A quadratic in $G$.  Solve:

$$
G(z) \;=\; \frac{(z - 1 + \gamma) - \sqrt{(z - 1 + \gamma)^2 - 4 \gamma z}}{2 \gamma z}.
$$

The discriminant factors as $(z - \lambda_-)(z - \lambda_+)$ with $\lambda_\pm = (1 \pm \sqrt{\gamma})^2$.  Apply Sokhotski-Plemelj for $\lambda \in (\lambda_-, \lambda_+)$, where the discriminant is negative and the imaginary part of $\sqrt{}$ is $\sqrt{-\text{disc}}$:

$$
\boxed{
\rho_{\text{MP}}(\lambda) \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{(\lambda_+ - \lambda)(\lambda - \lambda_-)}}{2\pi\, \gamma\, \lambda}, \qquad \lambda \in [\lambda_-, \lambda_+].
}
$$

This is the **Marchenko-Pastur density**.  Edges $\lambda_\pm = (1 \pm \sqrt{\gamma})^2$ as advertised.

When $\gamma > 1$, the matrix $\hat{\Sigma}$ has rank $n < p$ and so $p - n$ exact zero eigenvalues.  These contribute a delta of mass $1 - 1/\gamma$ at $\lambda = 0$ that we did not draw in §1.  The continuous part of the density is still the formula above; only the support $\lambda_-$ shifts away from zero again because $\sqrt{\gamma} > 1$.

```{ojs}
//| label: fig-mp-curves
//| fig-cap: "Marchenko-Pastur density as a function of $\\gamma = p / n$.  Pink dashed lines mark the edges $\\lambda_\\pm = (1 \\pm \\sqrt{\\gamma})^2$.  At $\\gamma = 1$ the lower edge $\\lambda_-$ touches $0$ and the density diverges as $1/\\sqrt{\\lambda}$ (integrable, but visible as a sharp spike near zero).  Density normalized to $\\int \\rho = 1$."

mp = (await fetch("data/section5_mp.json")).json()

viewof gamma_idx = Inputs.range([0, mp.gammas.length - 1], {
  step: 1,
  value: 19,
  label: "γ = p / n (concentration ratio)",
  format: i => mp.gammas[i].toFixed(3)
})

mp_curve = mp.curves[mp.gammas[gamma_idx].toFixed(3)]

Plot.plot({
  title: `Marchenko-Pastur density at γ = ${mp.gammas[gamma_idx].toFixed(3)}`,
  width: 720,
  height: 340,
  marginLeft: 60,
  marginBottom: 50,
  x: {label: "eigenvalue λ", domain: [0, 4.5]},
  y: {label: "density ρ_MP(λ)", grid: true, domain: [0, 2.5]},
  style: {background: "transparent", color: "#d8d6cf", fontSize: "13px"},
  marks: [
    Plot.areaY(
      mp_curve.x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: Math.min(mp_curve.y[i], 2.5)})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "#6cb0ff", fillOpacity: 0.4}
    ),
    Plot.line(
      mp_curve.x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: Math.min(mp_curve.y[i], 2.5)})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#f0c040", strokeWidth: 2.4}
    ),
    Plot.ruleX([mp_curve.lam_minus, mp_curve.lam_plus], {stroke: "#ff6c8e", strokeDasharray: "3,3"}),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Exercise 6.1
Verify directly from the formula that as $\gamma \to 0$, $\rho_{\text{MP}}$ becomes a delta at $\lambda = 1$.

<details><summary>Solution</summary>

For small $\gamma$, $\lambda_\pm = 1 \pm 2\sqrt{\gamma} + \gamma \approx 1 \pm 2\sqrt{\gamma}$.  The support is the narrow interval $[1 - 2\sqrt{\gamma}, 1 + 2\sqrt{\gamma}]$ of width $4\sqrt{\gamma} \to 0$.  Mass is conserved at $1$.  So the density localizes onto $\lambda = 1$, which is the classical limit.

</details>
:::

### Spike eigenvalues and the BBP transition

Real datasets are not pure noise.  They have signal: a few eigenvalues of the *true* covariance $\Sigma$ that are larger than $1$.  Such "spikes" can either be detectable -- pop above the upper edge $\lambda_+$ -- or get drowned in the bulk.  This is the **Baik-Ben Arous-Péché (BBP) transition**.

Consider $\Sigma = \mathrm{diag}(s, 1, 1, \ldots, 1)$, one spike of strength $s > 1$.  The empirical spectrum still has an MP bulk on $[\lambda_-, \lambda_+]$, plus possibly a single isolated eigenvalue near the top.  The BBP result says:

$$
\lambda_{\max}(\hat{\Sigma}) \;\to\; \begin{cases} \lambda_+ & \text{if } s \le 1 + \sqrt{\gamma}, \\ s + \dfrac{s\, \gamma}{s - 1} & \text{if } s > 1 + \sqrt{\gamma}. \end{cases}
$$

Below threshold the spike is "absorbed" into the bulk.  Above threshold it sticks out at a predictable location.

```{ojs}
//| label: fig-spike
//| fig-cap: "Sample-covariance spectrum with one true spike of strength $s$.  Bulk (blue histogram) sits inside the MP edges (pink dashes) regardless of $s$.  The orange tick marks the *empirical* mean of the largest eigenvalue across realizations.  Below the BBP threshold $s_c = 1 + \\sqrt{\\gamma}$, the spike is buried in the bulk; above it, the spike pops out and locks onto the analytic prediction (green tick)."

spk = (await fetch("data/section_spike.json")).json()

viewof spike_idx = Inputs.range([0, spk.spikes.length - 1], {
  step: 1,
  value: 10,
  label: "s (true-covariance spike strength)",
  format: i => spk.spikes[i].toFixed(2)
})

spkPanel = spk.panels[spk.spikes[spike_idx].toFixed(2)]

Plot.plot({
  title: `Spike-strength s = ${spk.spikes[spike_idx].toFixed(2)} (γ = ${spk.gamma.toFixed(2)}, BBP threshold s_c = ${spk.bbp_critical_spike.toFixed(3)})`,
  width: 720,
  height: 340,
  marginLeft: 60,
  marginBottom: 50,
  x: {label: "eigenvalue λ", domain: [0, Math.max(5, spkPanel.top_pred * 1.15)]},
  y: {label: "density ρ(λ)", grid: true, domain: [0, 1.5]},
  style: {background: "transparent", color: "#d8d6cf", fontSize: "13px"},
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(
      spkPanel.hist_x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: Math.min(spkPanel.hist_y[i], 1.5)})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "#6cb0ff", fillOpacity: 0.45, interval: spkPanel.hist_x[1] - spkPanel.hist_x[0]}
    ),
    Plot.line(
      spk.mp_x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: Math.min(spk.mp_y[i], 1.5)})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#f0c040", strokeWidth: 2.0, strokeDasharray: "5,3"}
    ),
    Plot.ruleX([1 - Math.sqrt(spk.gamma), spk.lam_plus].map(x => Math.max(x, 0)), {stroke: "#ff6c8e", strokeDasharray: "3,3"}),
    Plot.ruleX([spkPanel.top_mean], {stroke: "#f0c040", strokeWidth: 2.0}),
    Plot.ruleX([spkPanel.top_pred], {stroke: "#7be098", strokeWidth: 2.0}),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Try this
Drag $s$ from $1$ upward.  Below $s_c = 1 + \sqrt{\gamma} \approx 1.63$ (for $\gamma = 0.4$), the orange tick (empirical max eigenvalue) sits right at the upper edge $\lambda_+$ -- the spike is invisible.  Above $s_c$, the orange and green ticks (empirical and analytic predictions for the spike location) both jump out and track each other.  This is the practical meaning of "you need a strong enough signal to see it through high-dimensional noise."
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## A common confusion
You might think a true-covariance spike of $s = 1.5$ should be "obvious" since it's $50\%$ stronger than the ambient variance.  In low dimensions ($\gamma$ small), it is.  But for $\gamma = 0.5$, $s_c = 1 + \sqrt{0.5} \approx 1.71$, so $s = 1.5$ is *below threshold*: the empirical eigenvalues do not betray the existence of the spike.  More features means weaker spikes get drowned in noise.  This is why factor analysis on wide data is dangerous without a principled threshold.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Key idea
The Marchenko-Pastur density is the deterministic limit of sample-covariance eigenvalues when $p / n$ is held fixed.  Eigenvalues *inside* the bulk $[\lambda_-, \lambda_+]$ are noise; eigenvalues *outside* are signal.  The BBP transition fixes the detectability threshold for spikes.
:::

## 7.  Universality

Everything so far was derived for Gaussian matrices.  Here is the punchline that makes RMT useful: **the local statistics of eigenvalues do not depend on the entry distribution**.

Take any matrix $A$ with iid entries of mean zero, variance one, and (mild) finite higher moments, then symmetrize: $H = (A + A^\top)/\sqrt{2N}$.  Three things hold in the large-$N$ limit:

1. The bulk eigenvalue density is the **Wigner semicircle**.  No dependence on the entry distribution beyond mean and variance.
2. The nearest-neighbor spacings in the bulk follow the **Wigner surmise** corresponding to the symmetry class of $H$.
3. The fluctuations of the largest eigenvalue, after centering at $2$ and rescaling by $N^{2/3}$, follow the **Tracy-Widom distribution**.

This is **universality** -- the same statement, in spirit, as the central limit theorem.  Microscopic details (the entry distribution) wash out under coarse-graining; only macroscopic features (symmetry class, bulk density) survive.

::: {.column-margin}
The morally correct way to see universality is renormalization-group: the entry distribution sets a microscopic theory, and as we coarse-grain to look at long-wavelength eigenvalue correlations, all the irrelevant operators wash out.  The relevant data are the symmetry class and the macroscopic density.  CLT is the analogous RG fixed point on probability distributions; the semicircle is the fixed point on eigenvalue measures.
:::

### The β-ensembles

The symmetry class is encoded in a single integer-valued exponent $\beta$:

$$
P(\boldsymbol{\lambda}) \;\propto\; \prod_{i<j} |\lambda_i - \lambda_j|^{\beta} \,\cdot\, \prod_i e^{-\frac{\beta N}{4} \lambda_i^2}.
$$

| Class | $\beta$ | Matrix entries | Invariance |
|---|---|---|---|
| GOE | 1 | real | orthogonal |
| GUE | 2 | complex | unitary |
| GSE | 4 | quaternion | symplectic |

The exponent $\beta$ controls the strength of repulsion: $\beta = 1$ is "soft" (linear vanishing of the spacing distribution at zero), $\beta = 4$ is "hard" (quartic vanishing).  The Wigner surmise for the spacings:

$$
p_\beta(s) \;=\; a_\beta\, s^\beta\, e^{-b_\beta s^2}, \qquad
\begin{aligned}
\beta = 1: & \quad a = \pi/2, & b &= \pi/4, \\
\beta = 2: & \quad a = 32/\pi^2, & b &= 4/\pi, \\
\beta = 4: & \quad a = 262144/(729 \pi^3), & b &= 64/(9\pi).
\end{aligned}
$$

```{ojs}
//| label: fig-universality
//| fig-cap: "Wigner surmise for spacings in the three classical ensembles, with empirical histograms overlaid for $\\beta = 1$ (real GOE) and $\\beta = 2$ (complex GUE).  $\\beta = 4$ is the analytic surmise only.  Repulsion sharpens 1 → 2 → 4: the small-$s$ behavior is $s^\\beta$, so larger $\\beta$ means a more dramatic dead zone near zero.  Spacings normalized to mean 1."

uni = (await fetch("data/section6_universality.json")).json()

viewof beta_choice = Inputs.radio(
  new Map([
    ["β = 1 (GOE, real symmetric)", "1"],
    ["β = 2 (GUE, complex Hermitian)", "2"],
    ["β = 4 (GSE, quaternion self-dual)", "4"]
  ]),
  {label: "Symmetry class:", value: "2"}
)

uni_data = (() => {
  const sx = uni.surmise.x;
  const surmiseY = uni.surmise[`beta${beta_choice}`];
  const lines = sx.map((x, i) => ({x, y: surmiseY[i]}));
  const bars = uni.hist[`beta${beta_choice}`]
    ? uni.hist[`beta${beta_choice}`].x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: uni.hist[`beta${beta_choice}`].y[i]}))
    : [];
  return {lines, bars};
})()

Plot.plot({
  title: `Wigner surmise vs empirical spacings, β = ${beta_choice}`,
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 60,
  marginBottom: 50,
  x: {label: "spacing s / ⟨s⟩", domain: [0, 4]},
  y: {label: "density p(s)", grid: true, domain: [0, 1.4]},
  style: {background: "transparent", color: "#d8d6cf", fontSize: "13px"},
  marks: [
    uni_data.bars.length > 0
      ? Plot.rectY(uni_data.bars, {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "#6cb0ff", fillOpacity: 0.45,
                                   interval: uni.hist.beta1.x[1] - uni.hist.beta1.x[0]})
      : null,
    Plot.line(uni_data.lines, {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#f0c040", strokeWidth: 2.4}),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ].filter(d => d)
})
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Try this
Switch to $\beta = 1$.  The empirical histogram (light blue) and the Wigner surmise (orange) match.  Now switch to $\beta = 2$.  Same.  This is the basic universality test: whichever symmetry class you pick, the appropriate surmise is the right description of the observed spacings.

Even more striking: the empirical histogram at $\beta = 1$ is from a *Bernoulli-symmetric* matrix (entries $\pm 1$, no Gaussianness in sight) -- and it still matches the GOE surmise.  Universality means the distribution of entries is irrelevant.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Exercise 7.1
For a symmetric matrix with entries drawn iid from a Bernoulli distribution ($\pm 1/\sqrt{N}$), the bulk density is still the semicircle.  Explain in one sentence why this might be expected from a CLT-style argument.

<details><summary>Solution</summary>

The trace moments $\frac{1}{N}\mathbb{E}\,\mathrm{Tr}\,H^k$ depend only on the second moment of the entries (matched between Bernoulli and Gaussian) plus higher cumulants which contribute subleading-in-$N$ corrections.  Method-of-moments: matching all integer moments determines the limiting density, and only the variance survives in the leading-order computation.  This is exactly the CLT logic, applied to traces of polynomials of $H$ instead of sums of variables.

</details>
:::

### The Tracy-Widom distribution at the edge

Bulk universality controls the eigenvalues in the *interior* of the spectrum.  At the *edge* (the largest or smallest eigenvalue, or any place where the density vanishes like a square root) the relevant universal object is the **Tracy-Widom distribution**, $F_\beta$, which describes the fluctuations of the largest eigenvalue.

For GUE (and indeed any matrix with iid entries of variance $1/N$), the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}$ in our normalization concentrates at $2$ (the right edge of the semicircle), with fluctuations on scale $N^{-2/3}$:

$$
N^{2/3}(\lambda_{\max} - 2) \;\xrightarrow{d}\; \mathrm{TW}_\beta.
$$

Tracy and Widom showed that the limit is given by an integral involving the Painlevé II transcendent.  Most users take it on faith and use the tabulated CDF.  Below: empirical histograms of $N^{2/3}(\lambda_{\max} - 2)$ for several $N$, collapsing onto a master curve as $N$ grows.

```{ojs}
//| label: fig-tracy-widom
//| fig-cap: "Tracy-Widom convergence.  For each $N$ we sample many GUE matrices, take the largest eigenvalue, and apply the rescaling $\\xi = N^{2/3}(\\lambda_{\\max} - 2)$.  All four histograms should converge to the same master curve $\\mathrm{TW}_2$ (orange reference, computed empirically from $4{,}000$ realizations at $N = 400$).  Notice the asymmetry: the large-$\\xi$ tail is sharper than the small-$\\xi$ tail."

tw = (await fetch("data/section_tracy_widom.json")).json()

viewof tw_N = Inputs.range([0, tw.Ns.length - 1], {
  step: 1,
  value: 0,
  label: "N (matrix size)",
  format: i => tw.Ns[i]
})

twPanel = tw.panels[String(tw.Ns[tw_N])]

Plot.plot({
  title: `Distribution of N^(2/3) (λ_max − 2) at N = ${tw.Ns[tw_N]} (${twPanel.n_samples} samples)`,
  width: 720,
  height: 320,
  marginLeft: 60,
  marginBottom: 50,
  x: {label: "ξ = N^(2/3) (λ_max − 2)", domain: [-5, 3]},
  y: {label: "density", grid: true, domain: [0, 0.55]},
  style: {background: "transparent", color: "#d8d6cf", fontSize: "13px"},
  marks: [
    Plot.rectY(
      twPanel.x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: twPanel.y[i]})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "#6cb0ff", fillOpacity: 0.55, interval: twPanel.x[1] - twPanel.x[0]}
    ),
    Plot.line(
      tw.tw2_ref.x.map((x, i) => ({x, y: tw.tw2_ref.y[i]})),
      {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#f0c040", strokeWidth: 2.4}
    ),
    Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#ff6c8e", strokeDasharray: "3,3"}),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Try this
At $N = 50$ the empirical histogram is noticeably wider than the $N = 400$ reference; the rescaling $N^{2/3}$ has not fully kicked in.  By $N = 200$ the collapse is visible; by $N = 500$ it is essentially exact.  This is the slow approach: the relative error in the edge rescaling is $O(N^{-1/3})$, parametrically slower than the bulk's $O(N^{-1})$ corrections.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Key idea
Universality strips away every detail of the entry distribution except mean and variance, leaving a small set of universal limit objects: the bulk semicircle, the Wigner surmise (parametrized by $\beta$), the sine kernel for $k$-point correlations, and the Tracy-Widom distribution at the edge.  These appear in problems whose connection to matrices is far from obvious -- longest increasing subsequences, last-passage percolation, KPZ-class growth -- which is why RMT became a central tool across statistical physics, combinatorics, and number theory.
:::

## 8.  When to reach for RMT

A working signature: if your problem has many degrees of freedom that are coupled in some generic way, and you care about a **spectral quantity** (eigenvalues, singular values, condition numbers), the right tool is RMT or its non-commutative cousin, free probability.  Three patterns from machine learning:

### Sample covariance, PCA, and shrinkage

The most direct application.  Marchenko-Pastur tells you which empirical eigenvalues of $\hat{\Sigma}$ are noise (inside $[\lambda_-, \lambda_+]$) and which are signal (above $\lambda_+$).  Use it to:

- Set thresholds for spike detection in PCA, factor analysis, latent-variable models.
- Replace the classical "eigenvalues bigger than 1 are real" rule with an MP-aware threshold.
- Construct **shrinkage estimators** (Ledoit-Wolf, NERCOME) that pull empirical eigenvalues back toward the truth using the MP map.

::: {.column-margin}
There is a clean inversion: given an observed spike of empirical eigenvalue $\hat\lambda > \lambda_+$, the corresponding true-covariance eigenvalue is $\hat\lambda \cdot \frac{\hat\lambda - 1 - \gamma}{\hat\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{\hat\lambda - 1 - \gamma} \cdot \ldots$ -- a closed-form correction.  This is the basis of "high-dimensional PCA" estimators.
:::

### Hessians of trained networks

The Hessian of the loss at a trained neural network looks, empirically, like an MP bulk plus a few outlier eigenvalues.  Sagun et al. (2017) and many follow-ups document this phenomenology.  The bulk reflects the "noise" floor of pairwise weight interactions; the outliers reflect the actual loss-landscape curvature in the few directions that matter.  **Practical implication**: most second-order optimization methods are wasting time tracking the bulk, and Hessian-based generalization measures (sharpness, etc.) need to subtract off the MP bulk to be meaningful.

### Signal propagation in deep networks

Stacking many random weight matrices is the territory of **free probability**: the singular-value spectrum of the product of two independent random matrices is the **free multiplicative convolution** of the individual spectra.  At initialization, this controls whether forward signals and backward gradients explode or vanish.  The "edge of chaos" / "dynamical isometry" literature (Pennington et al.) is exactly this calculation: choose the per-layer spectrum so that the product spectrum has bounded condition number, and training works.

### What we did not cover

| Topic | Why it is interesting |
|---|---|
| Orthogonal-polynomial methods | The exact $N$-dependent computations: Hermite polynomials for GUE, Laguerre for Wishart.  Mehta @mehta2004 ch. 5. |
| Painlevé equations | Tracy-Widom is a Painlevé II transcendent; Wigner surmise is Painlevé V.  Tells you RMT touches integrable systems. |
| Free probability properly | The non-commutative analog of classical probability.  Voiculescu's framework.  Essential for random network calculations. |
| Ginibre and non-Hermitian RMT | When $H$ is not symmetric, eigenvalues live in $\mathbb{C}$ and have a circular law.  Important for dynamical systems, ecology. |
| Determinantal point processes | The right framework for the $k$-point correlation functions.  Sine kernel, Airy kernel. |
| Loop equations and topological recursion | The systematic large-$N$ expansion.  Connects RMT to 2D quantum gravity and matrix models. |

For all of those, Mehta's *Random Matrices* @mehta2004 is the canonical reference.  Tao's online notes are excellent for the modern probabilistic perspective.

::: {.callout-note}
## Final takeaway

The Vandermonde-squared in the joint eigenvalue density is the single non-classical fact in RMT.  Everything else follows from a Boltzmann-distribution-plus-saddle-point argument: the semicircle, Marchenko-Pastur, the Wigner surmise, BBP, Tracy-Widom.  Universality means these limits hold with no Gaussian assumption -- they are RG fixed points of the eigenvalue measure under coarse-graining.  When you see many coupled degrees of freedom and need a spectral statement, this is the toolbox.

The eigenvalues are repelling each other.  The rest is bookkeeping.
:::
